decision: Iron Law binds skill edits with no mechanical-edit exemption: a cell that edits any SKILL.md carries RED/GREEN pressure-test evidence, even when it only adds a numbered step invoking an already-verified command
rationale: Owner chose (a) at Gate 2 of the evolving loop (P18). Carving an exemption into the one law bee uses to protect itself from its own edits would be the first crack; bee-writing-skills already predicts the rationalization ('it is just one step'). Cost is bounded — a pressure scenario per skill edit — and the loop being built here is precisely the machinery that will edit skills at scale
alternatives: Mechanical-edit exemption for step-only additions that introduce no rule, discipline, or refusal (rejected by owner)

decision: Evolving loop design (P18, v2): (D1) dogfood repos stay zero-effort — digest is a compounding side effect; (D2 REVISED) the digest is an ALLOWLIST of structured fields — kind, layer, source, title, first_seen, pain — and carries NO free detail/text field, because measurement showed friction prose routinely names functions, files and config keys that no code-block strip or secret regex removes; (D2b) the CONSUMER revalidates and datamarks every foreign digest entry before it can enter a prompt, and every source path is realpath-contained; (D3) bee-evolving runs only in the bee repo, on demand; (D4) improvements go through the Iron Law (failing pressure test first, edits included); (D5) two human gates (what to fix, approve push) — push never auto
rationale: Validating slice A measured the real corpus across 5 bee repos and found the original D2 unenforceable: friction detail text is unfenced prose containing identifiers (readBacklogCounts, COMMAND_KEYS, approved_gates.shape, internal call graphs), which passes code-block stripping, passes the secret and injection regexes, and fits inside the 600-char cap. The plan had already shrunk D2 in prose without superseding it, violating its own prohibition. Owner chose the allowlist over a weakened promise: drop free text entirely rather than advertise a guarantee the pipeline cannot keep. D2b is added because the security panel showed the redaction boundary sat at the producer while bee-evolving — the consumer — is the party that edits its own source from foreign files; write-time scanning protects the wrong party, and a symlink under a foreign .bee/ defeats a string-prefix scope check

decision: Realpath containment is the only read-scope guard
rationale: Every filesystem read routes through resolveInScope(root, relPath) which path.resolve-normalizes then fs.realpath-validates against the repo root. fs.realpath rejects symlink escapes; a symlinked .bee/cells/evil.json pointing outside the repo is warned and never read. This is a read-scope invariant, not a sandbox. Bypassing it with fs.promises or named imports creates a silent escape hatch and must be prevented by source-level drift testing.

decision: Digest is an allowlist snapshot with no free-text fields
rationale: Each digest entry carries exactly six fields: kind, layer, source, title, first_seen, pain. No detail/text/outcome/deviations field. This removes surface cross-file dependencies from reachable data. A friction entry naming readBacklogCounts and COMMAND_KEYS leaves neither string in digest bytes. ENTRY_FIELDS, KIND_ALIASES, and DROP_REASONS are single-sourced and drift-tested to prevent accidental divergence. Import these constants rather than hardcoding copies in multiple modules.

decision: Foreign digest consumer revalidation: re-scan, datamark, allowlist enforcement
rationale: The producing repo's write-time scan does not protect the consumer. A hand-edited or hostile digest is just JSON on disk. For every foreign entry: resolve the path and reject symlink escapes by realpath; re-run SECRET_CONTENT_PATTERNS and INJECTION_PATTERNS dropping violators; wrap surviving titles in datamark() before they can enter a prompt (this prevents injection attacks); enforce ENTRY_FIELDS allowlist, never merging extra fields through. A missing or corrupt digest is warned and skipped, never thrown.

decision: A frozen assertion that encodes a defect is unfrozen by the planner/orchestrator, never by the worker: test_lib.mjs:1833 asserted a foreign entry's source survives mergeDigests raw ('src'), which IS the P1-1 vulnerability. Updating it to require datamarking is a strengthening, not a weakening, and the worker correctly returned [BLOCKED] rather than rewrite it
rationale: Cell evolving-3 wrote both the consumer-side boundary and the test that accepted its own gap; 93 green assertions therefore proved conformance to a wrong spec, not safety. Two opus reviewers reading the code missed that the suite blessed the defect — it surfaced only when a worker tried to fix the bug and the suite refused. The frozen-assertion rule must keep its escape hatch (worker STOPS and quotes the assertion) or the first defect encoded in a test becomes permanent
alternatives: Let the worker silently update the assertion (rejected: indistinguishable from weakening a test to make a change pass). Keep the assertion and drop the fix (rejected: ships the P1)

decision: Second narrow unfreeze (evolving-7): the ENTRY_FIELDS source-literal grep in test_lib.mjs:1421-1422 is deleted; the value-lock, the no-free-text check, and the SCHEMA_VERSION/DROP_REASONS literal pins all stay. A drift guard that regex-matches a module's own source pins SYNTAX, not behavior — and here the pinned syntax (a bare name array) was the defect itself, blocking the structural fix that makes forgetting a field impossible
rationale: Third occurrence of one defect class: mergeDigests neutralized only the fields someone remembered. The fix replaces ENTRY_FIELDS with ENTRY_FIELD_SPEC (field -> validator) and derives the name list from it, so an unspecced field cannot exist. The frozen grep demanded the literal array and thus forbade the fix. The repo already logged this exact anti-pattern against BACKLOG_STATUSES ('a value-lock, not a cross-file guard — regex-matching its own source guards nothing'), so this is a known, twice-seen failure mode, and the second worker to hit a defect-encoding assertion again correctly returned [BLOCKED] instead of rewriting it
alternatives: Keep the grep and patch only first_seen (rejected: field-by-field patching is what failed in rounds 1 and 2). Let the worker delete the assertion itself (rejected: indistinguishable from weakening a test to pass)

decision: Prefer an allowlist of structured fields over a blocklist filter whenever the data is human-authored prose: prose reliably embeds identifiers, and no strip or regex finds them. And place a redaction boundary at the party AT RISK, not the party producing the data — in a self-modifying or cross-trust pipeline, write-time validation protects the wrong party
rationale: Two independent surprises in P18 that neither the plan, the advisor, nor the first validation pass predicted. (1) Measurement showed the code-block strip removed exactly zero characters from real friction prose, which named functions, files, and config keys in ordinary sentences; the honest fix was deleting the field, not sharpening the filter. (2) The producing repo's write-time scan guards that repo against its own workers. bee-evolving reads foreign digests to edit bee's own source, so bee is the party at risk and must revalidate at read time — found only by a security lens explicitly asked who is at risk
alternatives: Sharpen the blocklist (rejected: measured no-op). Single write-time boundary (rejected: protects the producer, not the consumer)

decision: Intake gate fires in every terminal phase (idle + compounding-complete), and the guard is doctrinally a safety net, not the authority
rationale: A closed feature left phase=compounding-complete with gates still approved: the idle branch missed the phase, the gated branch saw execution:true, and every post-feature source edit fell through to allow — observed in a real host session (assets/css/tasks.css edited with no routing). Fix is TERMINAL_PHASES in checkWrite plus AGENTS critical rule 12 / hive law 13, because the code hole and the 'hook didn't block me so it's allowed' reasoning are the same failure seen from two sides.

decision: auto-approved Gates 1-3 (bypass) for fanout-doctrine: promote the Delegation contract (fan-out rubric) from references/routing-and-contracts.md into AGENTS.block.md critical rules, so it lives in the always-loaded doctrine layer
rationale: The rule exists but sits in a reference file only read when a bee skill is invoked; in a plain conversation turn no instruction to fan out reaches the agent, so the owner has to repeat it by hand. Owner calls fan-out a core setup. Small lane: 3 files (template, root AGENTS.md via re-onboard, census test).

decision: Doctrine that must hold in EVERY turn — including plain conversation turns where no bee skill routes — belongs in AGENTS.block.md, not in a skill's references/ file. A reference is loaded only on skill invoke; a rule parked there is silently absent exactly when no skill is running.
rationale: The Delegation contract (fan-out: >3 files or digest-only -> down-tier I/O worker) was fully specified in routing-and-contracts.md and cited by every skill, yet the owner still had to repeat 'delegate file search to subagents' by hand — because conversation turns never load that reference. Placement, not phrasing, was the defect. Generalizes: for any always-applies rule, ask 'does this need to hold when no skill is running?' — if yes, it goes in the doctrine block.

decision: A switch that narrows what an upgrade COMPARES must equally narrow what it CLAIMS. --repo-hooks scoped both the refresh and the up-to-date comparison, so omitting it made stale guards invisible to the drift check and the run still reported up_to_date. The opt-in is now sticky (recorded repo_hooks forces the refresh).
rationale: 8 host repos ran current doctrine against first-onboard guards across several versions — missing adapter.mjs and bee-model-guard.mjs entirely, and a 195-line write-guard where source had 435. Nothing surfaced it: the guard files were excluded from the comparison exactly when they were excluded from the refresh. Generalizes to every future flag: a partial upgrade that reports success is worse than one that fails.

decision: Promote the explicit-tier transport requirement (model param / anchored [bee-tier:] marker, decision 0023) into critical rule 13 of AGENTS.block.md
rationale: Rule 13 orders fan-out in every turn including plain conversation turns, but the HOW (transport) lived only in bee-hive's references/routing-and-contracts.md, which loads only when a skill runs. An always-loaded rule whose mechanics live in an on-demand reference produces a predictable first-dispatch deny from bee-model-guard in host repos (anphabe-gogl). decision: Tracked paths must be checkout-able on Windows (no colon, asterisk, question mark, quote, angle bracket or pipe; no reserved DOS names; no trailing dot/space) - enforced by scripts/test_portable_paths.mjs inside the repo verify. install.ps1 now clones with --no-checkout + sparse-checkout of skills/ and .claude-plugin/ only, then probes for onboard_bee.mjs, so no ref (old tags included) can yield a silent empty source.
rationale: A tracked doc named with a colon aborted git checkout on NTFS (invalid path); the clone stayed empty and install.ps1 reported it as a network failure. Two layers: kill the bad path and guard it, and narrow what the installer ever checks out.

decision: D1 — Scope: the full package ships as one feature: measurement (tools.jsonl logger + the FACT-E probe spike), the two guard-hole fixes, pinned agent types, the advisor protocol, AND worker-ising cell execution for every lane including tiny.
rationale: Worker-ising execution is the only part with leverage on the bill (cost = turns x context, not bytes read), and the user asked explicitly for orchestration that plans rather than lunges. The other four parts make the system honest and safe but move no money; shipping them alone would leave the actual problem untouched.
alternatives: Defer worker-ising to a follow-up until the logger produces real numbers (safer, but the invoice would not change this round); or sequence it last within the same feature.

decision: D2 — Advisor triggers, v1: all four. (a) two consecutive red verifies on the same cell; (b) hard-gate/high-risk work before Gate 3; (c) two locked decisions in conflict; (d) cell scope creeping past the plan's declared file list.
rationale: Triggers must be observable EVENTS, never the model's felt uncertainty: self-assessed doubt is exactly what a context-pressured model is worst at, and betting on it repeats the failure of the prose fan-out rubric. (a) is the strongest and cheapest — wrong twice in the same direction predicts a third wrong turn in that direction, and it is already countable from state with no self-report.
alternatives: Ship only (a) in v1 and add the rest later. Carried caveat for planning: (c) conflict-detection is semantic rather than countable, so it needs a cheap mechanical proxy and an explicit false-positive budget.

decision: D3 — A fired trigger blocks at the CLOSE POINTS, never mid-work: cells cap refuses a cell that has gone red twice without an advisor record, and Gate 3 does not open for hard-gate work without an advisor_ref. Work continues in the meantime.
rationale: This removes the choice at a discrete event instead of policing a continuous one. A hard mid-work block would teach the model to act until a hook stops it — the precise inversion of critical rule 12 that got the byte-budget hook rejected in the same conversation.
alternatives: Hard block the moment a trigger fires (cuts the third wrong step earliest, but inverts rule 12); or advisory-only (which is the reminder this whole design concluded does not work).

decision: D4 — The advisor call path is split by trigger class. A worker calls the advisor CLI directly for the technical dead-ends it owns (two red verifies, scope creep), and the answer rides home inside its digest. The orchestrator calls the advisor for decision-shaped triggers (hard-gate before Gate 3, conflicting locked decisions).
rationale: The triggers do not live in one place: a worker holds the problem context but holds neither the gates nor the decision log. Forcing every consult up to the orchestrator would make it reload the technical context the worker already has — spending exactly the context this feature exists to save. A Claude Code worker cannot spawn a subagent, but it can run a shell command, and bee's advisor slot is already an external CLI, so the worker path is available today.
alternatives: Worker always returns [ADVISOR-NEEDED] and the orchestrator consults (cleanest accountability, extra round-trip, wastes orchestrator context); or worker always self-consults (simplest, but silently drops triggers (b) and (c), which a worker cannot see).

decision: D5 — The advisor MUST be cross-family with the ceiling model. Config validation refuses an advisor whose model family matches ceiling, and says why.
rationale: The advisor's value is decorrelated error, not extra intelligence. Asking Opus from Opus returns your own blind spots back with a confident signature — a paid ritual that nods. Making this a mechanism rather than a note is the same remove-the-choice rule as D3.
alternatives: Allow an explicit advisor.allow_same_family override for single-provider shops; or ship it as advice plus a bee status warning (rejected: that is the reminder this design concluded is useless).

decision: D6 — Guiding principle, adopted repo-wide: mechanisms that REMOVE a choice are lean; mechanisms that PUNISH a choice are ceremony wearing enforcement's clothes. Hooks are good at discrete events and worst at continuous resources. Not a wall, a floor. Corollary, explicitly NOT built: any byte- or token-budget hook throttling Read/Grep, and any check that the model name must appear in the Agent description.
rationale: A pinned agent definition leaves nothing to forget, so there is nothing to enforce. A byte budget leaves the choice in place and adds a rule to manage plus an invented threshold; it meters the second-order term (bytes) while the bill is driven by the first-order one (turns x context); it is routed around by a well-meaning model via Bash cat; and it teaches read-until-blocked, which makes 'an unblocked read is not an approved read' false. The description check is theater — it enforces a string nobody validates, and pinned agent types subsume it.
alternatives: Build the byte-budget hook as originally proposed (rejected on all five counts above after an adversarial cross-family advisor review); build the description check as a cheap display fix (rejected as redundant).

decision: AO2 — Advisor triggers. The worker-level trigger is NOT changed: the shipped rule stands (consult on the FIRST serious failed verify, budget 2 per claim, exhausted budget ends in [BLOCKED] and the cell never reaches cap). This feature ADDS the triggers that do not exist: (b) hard-gate / high-risk work before Gate 3, and (c) two locked decisions in conflict — both orchestrator-level, which bee-executing/SKILL.md:74 explicitly excludes today. Trigger (d), cell scope creeping past a declared file list, is DROPPED from v1: cells carry no expected file list at claim time, so it has no source of truth.
rationale: The original D2 was written on the false premise that nothing triggers the advisor. The shipped worker loop is in fact STRICTER than what was proposed: it consults one attempt earlier and closes at [BLOCKED] rather than at cap. Adopting the proposal would have been a regression that also required a red-streak counter that does not exist (cells.mjs recordVerify overwrites a single scalar verify_passed with no history).

decision: AO3 — Enforcement point, for the NEW orchestrator-level triggers only: Gate 3 does not open for hard-gate/high-risk work without an advisor_ref. Work is never blocked mid-flight. The worker-level close point stays exactly as shipped ([BLOCKED] on an exhausted consult budget) and is not moved to cap.
rationale: Same remove-the-choice-at-a-discrete-event principle as before, but applied only where a gap actually exists. The original D3 would have relocated the shipped worker close point from [BLOCKED] to cap — strictly weaker, and unnecessary.

decision: AO4 — The worker advisor path already exists and is kept verbatim (Advisor line in the dispatch, degenerate check run by the orchestrator at dispatch, model-shaped or cli-shaped transport, consults recorded in the cap trace). What this feature adds is the ORCHESTRATOR path — the advisor consulted at Gate 3 and on a decision conflict — which bee-executing/SKILL.md:74 today explicitly rules out.
rationale: The split-by-trigger-class conclusion survives, but half of it turned out to be already built. The genuinely new half is the half the user asked for: the orchestrator itself must be able to consult.

decision: AO5 — The advisor must be cross-family with the runtime, enforced at config-validate time: under runtime  a plain claude model-string advisor is same-family by construction and is REFUSED with a stated reason. An explicit  flag on the advisor slot overrides the refusal for shops that cannot install a foreign CLI; when the flag is on,  warns.
rationale: Cross-family is what buys decorrelated error, and config keys are already runtime-scoped (models.claude.advisor), so same-family is decidable at config time — no need to compare against , which decision 0015 says is never configured (the flaw that made the original D5 unimplementable as written). The override exists because strict enforcement would make an external CLI a hard dependency for using the advisor at all, and would kill the shipped model-shaped advisor transport outright.

decision: AO5 — The advisor must be cross-family with the runtime, enforced at config-validate time: under runtime "claude", an advisor given as a plain claude model string is same-family by construction and is REFUSED with a stated reason. An explicit allow_same_family flag on the advisor slot overrides the refusal, for shops that cannot install a foreign CLI; while that flag is on, bee status warns.
rationale: Restates AO5 without shell backticks, which silently ate four words in the first record. Substance unchanged: cross-family is what buys decorrelated error; config keys are already runtime-scoped (models.claude.advisor) so same-family is decidable at config time — no need to compare against ceiling, which decision 0015 says is never configured, and that flaw is what made the original D5 unimplementable. The override exists because strict enforcement would make a foreign CLI a hard dependency for using the advisor at all, and would kill the shipped model-shaped advisor transport outright.

decision: AO7 — Decision IDs for this feature are prefixed AO (AO1..AO8), not D. The earlier advisor feature already cites D1/D2/D3 verbatim inside live code and skills, so a second D1 in the same area would make every downstream citation ambiguous.
rationale: Found by the fresh-eyes review: state.mjs:740 cites "Decision D2 (advisor feature)", bee-swarming/SKILL.md:42 cites D1/D2, cell adv-2 is titled "D3 loop", and routing-and-contracts.md:195 carries yet another D1. Renumbering costs nothing now and is unfixable later.

decision: AO8 — The advisor runs READ-ONLY. The advisor command in this repo today is "codex exec ... --yolo ... workspace-write", i.e. it can write. Advice-only is already the law (bee-executing: advice never authorizes a package install, a gate approval, or file scope beyond the cell) but nothing enforces it at the transport, so the config is one careless advisor away from a writer. Bring the advisor slot under the same read-only rule the review slot already obeys.
rationale: Bee already requires review-class slots to be read-only and grants write access only to generation. The advisor is advisory by definition, so it belongs on the read-only side; leaving it workspace-write means the mechanism that is supposed to catch a mistake can itself make one. Surfaced by the fresh-eyes review as an unnumbered rule stated in prose.

decision: AO5 (replaces the cross-family rule) — CONFIG IS THE AUTHORITY; THE MODEL DOES NOT GET A VOTE. The configured advisor IS the advisor: it is consulted whenever a trigger fires, with no family test, no strength test, and no self-judged skip. The same rule generalises to tiers: a dispatch declaring generation or extraction MUST run on the model configured for that tier; a same-family fallback is chosen ONLY when that tier is unconfigured. Consequence: the degenerate check at bee-swarming/SKILL.md:43-45, which ranks models by a hardcoded haiku<sonnet<opus order and silently SKIPS the configured advisor when it judges it not stronger, is removed — narrowed at most to the one honest no-op case (the advisor resolves to literally the same model as the worker). Consequence for the guard: bee-model-guard must validate that the model param EQUALS the model configured for the declared tier, not merely that it is a non-empty string.
rationale: Owner decision, 2026-07-14, and it is a better frame than the cross-family rule it replaces. Cross-family was a proxy for "the advisor should actually be a second opinion"; the owner named the real principle underneath: the repo owner configured a model on purpose, and no runtime heuristic gets to overrule that. The hardcoded strength ladder is the model second-guessing its owner. This also dissolves the AO4/AO5 conflict the round-2 review found (enforcing cross-family under the claude runtime would have made every model-shaped advisor unreachable), needs no allow_same_family escape hatch, and gives the guard-hole fix a principled definition of "valid" instead of an allowlist of model names.

decision: AO4 — Call paths. The worker advisor loop is kept as shipped (Advisor line in the dispatch, first-serious-failure trigger, budget 2 per claim, model- or cli-shaped transport, consults recorded in the cap trace, [BLOCKED] on exhaustion) EXCEPT that the orchestrator no longer second-guesses the configured advisor at dispatch (per AO5): if an advisor is configured, the Advisor line is added. What this feature adds is the orchestrator path — the advisor consulted at Gate 3 for hard-gate work and on a decision conflict — which bee-executing/SKILL.md:74 today explicitly rules out.
rationale: Restates AO4 under AO5. The split-by-trigger-class conclusion stands: a worker holds the problem context but holds neither the gates nor the decision log. The only change is that the degenerate check stops being a veto the model exercises over the owner configuration.

decision: AO9 — AO2(c) "two locked decisions in conflict" is NOT dropped, and NOT built in v1: it is deferred behind a prerequisite feature, "structured-decisions", which gives decision records a machine-readable claim (subject / predicate / value) so conflict becomes mechanically detectable. advisor-and-orchestration ships with the orchestrator trigger AO2(b) only — hard-gate/high-risk before Gate 3. structured-decisions gets its own exploring: it is a data-model change to an append-only log with 135 existing events (schema, migration, CLI shape, compatibility with activeDecisions/supersede).
rationale: Validating proved AO2(c) has no mechanical detector against the CURRENT record shape: decision and rationale are free prose with no structured claim; scope is the default "repo" in 106 of 135 events and is absent entirely from supersede events; and the only mechanical relation in the store (supersedes) is the RESOLUTION of a conflict, not its detection — a superseded decision is no longer active, so two active decisions can never be in a machine-visible contradiction. Any proxy (keyword overlap, same-scope pair, embedding similarity) would fire constantly on the 106 same-scope records, and a flaky trigger is strictly worse than none because it destroys the rarity that is the advisor s entire value. The owner declined to drop the trigger and chose to build the foundation instead; SPLIT RECOMMENDED was answered rather than silently shrinking a locked decision.
alternatives: Drop AO2(c) from v1 entirely (rejected by the owner). Keep it as a manually-invoked trigger (rejected: that is "the model notices", which this whole design concluded does not work). Fold structured-decisions into this feature as a slice (rejected: a data-model change inside an already-6-workstream high-risk feature would swallow it).

decision: AO10 — .claude/agents/ MUST NOT be added as a third entry to REPO_SKILL_TARGETS (onboard_bee.mjs:256-259). It ships through a separate flat managed-file sync with its own version marker, the same class as the .claude/settings.json hook merge.
rationale: Validating found this would brick onboarding permanently and non-forceably on every host: the three-version preflight resolves a target version by reading <targetRoot>/bee-hive/s version marker, and an agents root has no bee-hive DIRECTORY — a target with any bee-* presence but no readable bee-hive marker resolves as "unknown", which is refused and is never forceable. Additionally skillSyncTargets + SKILL_DIR_RE assume DIRECTORIES named bee-*, while agents are FILES (bee-gather.md), so the dir-scan finds nothing; and the deletion-root guard resolves its root from the same array and fails closed on an unknown kind. plan.md proposed exactly the forbidden change.

decision: AO11 — Codex gets an explicit documented ASYMMETRY on pinned agent types, not parity. Codex has no per-agent model selection (DEFAULT_MODELS.codex is all-null by design, state.mjs:96-97) and enforces a tier as a read budget + output cap in the worker prompt instead. A model: pinned in agent frontmatter is a no-op file for that runtime.
rationale: Faking parity would ship a file that does nothing and imply an enforcement that does not exist. The asymmetry is real, it is already how bee models Codex tiers, and documenting it is honest; hiding it would be the drift.

decision: AO12 — AO5 config validation is hosted in `bee status`, backed by a shared validator in state.mjs and also exposed as a `bee config validate` verb for CI. resolveTier is explicitly DISQUALIFIED as the host.
rationale: resolveTier is called from inside hooks, and every bee hook is fail-open by contract (catch -> return 0). A throw there would be swallowed by the hook s own catch and logged as a crash — a refusal that refuses nothing — and it would put a throw on the write-guard s hot path. Onboarding alone is also disqualified: it runs once, and .bee/config.json is hand-edited afterwards. `bee status --json` is the mandatory first call of every session and every compaction (AGENTS.md startup step 3) and already reads the models block, so a hard error there is seen before any cell is claimed. Concrete damage today: normalizeTierValue returns undefined for any unrecognised shape and the key is silently DROPPED — one typo ({"advisor": {"modle": "gpt-5"}}) disables the advisor entirely and nothing anywhere says so.

decision: AO13 — An advisor_ref is STALE if any of: its feature differs from state.feature; the newest active decision id has changed since the consult; the sha256 of plan.md has changed; or the ref predates the most recent revocation of the execution gate. Explicitly NOT a time-based TTL. Gate 3 checks it as a precondition inside handleStateGate: when --name execution --approved true AND state.mode is high-risk, require a non-stale advisor_ref for state.feature, else throw with the reason and a FIX line. It must throw, never warn.
rationale: A TTL is an invented number, and this feature already burned itself on one ("two red verifies"). Every staleness condition above is a real event with a real record. mode is already in state.json, and hard-gate work is DEFINED as routing to high-risk, so mode === high-risk is a sound cheap proxy that over-covers in the safe direction with zero false positives on strictness. A warning is the anti-pattern AO3 exists to escape (STALE_ADVISOR_NOTICE is exactly that).

decision: AO14 — The LANE TABLE gives way; AO1 stands. skills/bee-hive/SKILL.md:116 Execute column for the tiny lane changes from "direct, in-session (solo)" to a dispatched worker, and skills/bee-swarming/SKILL.md:22 ("no workers are spawned" for tiny/small) is amended in the same slice. Consequential (agent discretion, exercised here): the ORCHESTRATOR authors the done-report, and its evidence is the worker verbatim diff PLUS the orchestrators own independent re-run of the verify command — not the worker word. The Delegation contract gains a second, named class of worker: an EXECUTION worker, which unlike an I/O-offload worker DOES register in the swarm registry and DOES take reservations.
rationale: The ao-spike-tiny spike returned a bare NO with the loophole closed on its own initiative: a dispatched worker fails all three words of "direct, in-session (solo)" independently, the "I/O-offload workers exempt" parenthetical sits in the Validate column not Execute, and the Delegation contract defines an I/O worker as a digest-returning gather that explicitly does NOT register in the swarm registry — so a cell-executing worker is, by that contracts own line, not an I/O worker. AO1 and the lane table were therefore in direct contradiction and one had to give. The owner chose the lane table, because worker-ising tiny execution is the ONLY part of this feature with leverage on the bill (cost = turns x context; the tiny lane is precisely where the orchestrator reads, edits and tests inside its own window) and because it is what carries the advisor down into the lane bee runs most often. On the done-report: the orchestrator already MUST re-run every verify itself under the goal-check rule (P12/decision 0018), so naming it the author changes nothing mechanical — it only stops the contract from pretending the executing agent writes it.
alternatives: Amend AO1 instead and drop the tiny clause (rejected by the owner: the invoice would not move, and the tiny lane would stay advisor-blind). Worker-ise small only and wait for logger data before touching tiny (rejected: most real work runs in the tiny lane, so it saves far less).

decision: A drift failure has two sides, and we assumed the wrong one. Both the session model and a two-lens review panel diagnosed test_hook_contracts.mjs 22 failures as a rotted fixture (staging wrappers at the wrong path), by pattern-matching the standing fixture-rot learning. The fixture was already correct and already used readdirSync; its diff is zero. The real defect was .codex/hooks.json — a GENERATED, TRACKED file that had been hand-edited (commit 744b1bec) to .bee/bin/hooks/ paths, while hooks/catalog.mjs has always rendered hooks/${script} for the codex repo target. Regenerating it from the catalog fixed all 22 rows. RULE: when a drift check fails, establish WHICH side drifted from git history before repairing either; never assume the test is the stale one. Corollary: a generated file that is tracked can be hand-edited, and the drift check is the only thing that will ever notice.
rationale: A standing learning (critical-patterns.md:6-18, fixture file-lists rot) was fresh in context and matched the shape of the symptom, so three independent readers reached for it. The worker doing the actual repair ran git log -p --follow and found the opposite. Pattern-matching a recent learning is exactly how a correct-looking wrong diagnosis survives review: the panel was adversarial about the PLAN and credulous about the DIAGNOSIS. The cheap mechanical defence is to make the drift-side determination a required step, not an inference.

decision: AO15 — A PreToolUse payload DOES distinguish the orchestrator from a subagent, and the tools-logger agent column IS buildable. Proven by an orchestrator-side control capture: on an orchestrator tool call the payload carries NO agent_id and NO agent_type (the fields are absent); on a subagent tool call both are present, and agent_type names the agent type verbatim (e.g. "Explore"). session_id is IDENTICAL across both, so session_id is NOT the discriminator — presence/absence of agent_id is. The full payload also carries prompt_id, permission_mode, effort, tool_use_id and transcript_path, none of which bee reads today. This SUPERSEDES the NO verdict returned by cell ao-spike-probe, which was correct on the data it had: a dispatched worker structurally cannot execute a tool call as the top-level orchestrator, so it could never obtain the control half of the comparison. Also disproven empirically: the assumption that Claude Code snapshots hooks at session start and a mid-session binding is inert — the binding fired on the very next Read.
rationale: The spike was designed to fail safe, and it did: it returned NO rather than fabricate the missing half, and it said in its own report that the orchestrator should run its own side directly. The orchestrator did exactly that (disposable probe, gitignored paths, whitelist capture widened to record agent_type verbatim — a closed-vocabulary type name, not a secret — and a hashed agent_id), and the answer inverted. NOTE for the record: this does NOT revive the byte-budget hook. AO6 rejected it on five grounds and only ONE of them was "we cannot tell orchestrator from subagent". The other four stand: wrong metric (cost is turns x context, not bytes), trivially routed around by Bash cat, an invented threshold, and it inverts critical rule 12 by teaching read-until-blocked. A newly available mechanism is not a reason to build a thing that was rejected on its merits.

decision: AO16 — Slice 2 splits into 2A (make a cli-shaped tier actually work for gathers) and 2B (AO5 model-equality + degenerate-check removal + AO8 read-only advisor). Nothing is dropped; 2B keeps every locked clause and gets its own gate. Owner approved the split at Gate 2 ('Duyet — tach 2A/2B nhu de xuat').
rationale: The owner's config exercises a cli-shaped GENERATION tier — an out-of-family model doing GATHER work — a path the plan never modelled. 2B cannot ride along because AO5 requires the model param to equal the model configured for the DECLARED tier, but the guard's two transports are alternatives: a dispatch may carry a model param with NO marker, leaving no declared tier to compare against. Enforcing AO5 literally forces a second, larger decision (must every dispatch now carry a marker?) — a change to the transport contract itself, not a hole-plug.
alternatives: Ship W1+W2+W6 together as originally shaped — rejected: it silently entangles the marker-mandatory question with a hole-plug the owner asked for today.

decision: AO17 — The cli-executor invocation contract is: run the configured command VERBATIM, prompt on stdin, capture stdout; append NOTHING. And every path handed to a cli worker is ABSOLUTE. Supersedes swarming-reference.md:85, which appends '-o <file> -' to the command.
rationale: Measured against the owner's real executor (agy) this session, four probes: (1) reads a repo file by relative path — PASS; (2) writes by relative path via its file tool — replied WROTE, exit 0, and created NO file anywhere on disk (a silent success-report, the exact class decision 0019's accept-by-artifact rule exists to catch); (3) writes by relative path via its shell tool — the file was really created inside /home/thanhsmind/.gemini/antigravity-cli/scratch, i.e. the external CLI's cwd is NOT the repo root and bee cannot make it so; (4) writes by ABSOLUTE path — PASS, real file in the repo. Appending flags breaks any non-codex-shaped command outright (with 'bash -lc ...' the -o lands on bash). Relative paths misfire silently under a sandboxing CLI: the .bee/bin helpers would touch a phantom .bee/ in the CLI's scratch while the worker reports done.
alternatives: Keep appending codex flags and document the limitation — rejected: it makes bee's argv shape the authority over the owner's config, which is the exact inversion AO5 forbids.

decision: AO18 — The owner keeps --dangerously-skip-permissions on the cli GATHER command, with the risk stated and accepted. This is a deliberate, logged exception to swarming-reference.md:91 ('never a machine-wide bypass as the house default') and to AO8's read-only-advice-slot rule, not an oversight.
rationale: Surfaced at Gate 2 with the evidence: probe 4 proved the flag is not decorative — with an absolute path the gather worker can write anywhere the owner can, and a gather needs no write access at all. Owner's answer, verbatim: 'Giu nguyen — toi biet rui ro'. Logged so a future reader finds an accepted risk, not a missed one.
alternatives: Hunt for a read-only/sandbox flag on agy and narrow the generation command — offered and declined by the owner.

decision: AO19 — AO17 is CORRECTED, not confirmed. The trailing '-' in the old dispatch line is NOT an appended convenience flag: per 'codex exec --help', the bare positional IS the prompt, and '-' is what makes codex read the prompt from STDIN instead. So 'run the configured command verbatim, append nothing' would silently stop every codex-shaped command lacking its own '-' from ever receiving its prompt. bee must not append -o; but the prompt TRANSPORT must become an explicit, validated property of the configured command, not an assumption.
rationale: Caught by the coherence panel and re-verified by the orchestrator against codex exec --help: '[PROMPT] Initial instructions for the agent. If not provided as an argument (or if - is used), [read from stdin]'. AO17 as logged would have shipped a contract that breaks the advisor and the codex review slot.

decision: AO20 — Decision 0019's 'first dogfood pending' is CLOSED, and the answer changes the risk picture. bee's external path DID run end to end for the first time (2026-07-14): codex exec -m gpt-5.6-sol -s read-only -c model_reasoning_effort=high -  , prompt on stdin, correct answer, exit 0. It needed NO --yolo and NO write access. Separately, agy --sandbox --mode plan (no --dangerously-skip-permissions) also read the repo and answered correctly. CONCLUSION: the machine-wide bypass bee was shipping as its default preset was never a requirement — it was surplus privilege.
rationale: The reason the dogfood stayed pending for four days was not effort: the shipped command was malformed. 'workspace-write' sat in the [PROMPT] positional (codex exec --help), -s was missing, and --yolo (= --dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox) meant no sandbox ever ran. The advisor had been receiving the literal string 'workspace-write' as its question. A broken command reported nothing; nobody noticed, because advice is prose and prose always looks like an answer.
alternatives: Keep --yolo and only add -s — rejected: measured, read-only answers the question just as well, and AO8 already locks advice-class slots to read-only.

decision: chain-integrity D1 — lock the chain's TAIL, not the whole phase machine: scribing only from swarming, compounding only from scribing, compounding-complete only from compounding. Every other transition stays permissive; moving BACKWARD stays legal.
rationale: The observed failure was a hand-typed 'state set --phase compounding-complete' straight from swarming, which the enum-only check (state.mjs:18, bee.mjs:752-779) happily allowed — there is no from->to legality check anywhere in the repo. Locking the tail makes that exact call refuse.
alternatives: A full from->to table over all 10 phases: REJECTED. An over-strict rail reliably teaches the agent to route around it — the same failure in a new costume. Hive law 5 also requires backward moves (failed reality gate / NO spike -> back to planning) to stay legal.

decision: chain-integrity D2 — scribing debt becomes fail-close at exactly ONE boundary: entering compounding-complete is refused while scribingDebt() > 0, and the refusal names each capped behavior_change cell still owing its spec sync. Everywhere else it stays advisory.
rationale: cells.mjs:521 states the posture verbatim — 'Pure read — never a blocker, only a signal' — and bee-compounding/SKILL.md:77 says 'Prose-ruled, never hook-enforced (D7)'. That is precisely why last_scribing_run=None alongside six capped behavior_change cells was a fully VALID bee state; nothing in the system considered it wrong. Debt should be a signal through the work and a wall at the door.
alternatives: Blocking on debt everywhere (at cap, at claim): rejected as too noisy — it would fire mid-work when the spec sync is not yet due.

decision: chain-integrity D5 — gate_bypass stays ON and is NOT touched by this feature.
rationale: Verified in code: no guard, hook, claimCell, or checkWrite branches on gate_bypass; it is read in exactly two display-only places (bee.mjs:299, inject.mjs:164). Under bypass the agent records the SAME state write a human 'yes' would produce, which is why the mechanical guards saw nothing unusual. Bypass removed the HUMAN joints of the chain, but those joints were made of habit, not code. It decides WHO approves gates 1-3; it never decides WHETHER the chain's tail ran.
alternatives: Turning bypass off: rejected — it would hide this failure, not fix it. The chain could break identically, just more slowly, with the user paying three approval clicks per feedback round to buy nothing.

decision: chain-integrity D3 — 'state scribing-run' stops jumping the phase blindly: it currently sets phase='compounding' unconditionally (bee.mjs:984-998) with no check the phase was 'scribing'. It must obey the same tail rule as everything else rather than bypass it.
rationale: A handler that writes a phase through a private path is exactly the kind of side door that made the original failure invisible. One choke point or none.
alternatives: Exempting scribing-run as 'the blessed writer': rejected — a blessed writer is a hole with a nicer name.

decision: chain-integrity D4 — the debt override is LOUD and LOGGED, never silent: closing a feature that legitimately owes no spec (owner judges a behavior_change cell spec-irrelevant) requires an explicit flag that logs a decision. Never a default, never a silent skip.
rationale: Fail-close with no escape hatch produces an agent that learns to hack the state file. Fail-close with a silent hatch produces the failure we just had. Fail-close with a loud, attributable hatch is the only shape that survives contact with a real session.
alternatives: No override at all: rejected (invites working around the guard, cf. hive law 13 — an unblocked write is not an approved write, and a guard with no door gets a hole punched in it).

decision: chain-integrity D1-REVISED — CORRECTS D1. Guard the DOOR, not the phase name. 'state set --phase compounding' is refused outright (use 'state scribing-run'); 'state scribing-run' is the SOLE producer of phase=compounding and itself requires the current phase be swarming/reviewing/scribing; 'compounding-complete' requires phase=compounding AND zero scribing debt. 'scribing' stays permissive — it is a marker, not a gate.
rationale: Validation killed the original D1. Grep proves NOTHING in the entire repo ever sets --phase scribing (zero hits): bee-scribing/SKILL.md:112 goes straight to 'state scribing-run', which hard-codes phase='compounding' at bee.mjs:996. So D1's 'compounding only from scribing' would have made compounding UNREACHABLE and refused the only scribing path that exists. Guarding the door instead of the phase name enforces the same thing more strongly: the terminal is reachable only by actually RECORDING a scribing run, because scribing-run is the sole door.
alternatives: Placing the rule in writeState (state.mjs:291): REJECTED. It is a raw writeJsonAtomic passthrough with zero validation, and ~60 test fixtures plus the hooks write phase straight through it. A rule there breaks the fixtures and the hooks; a rule at the CLI handlers (handleStateSet / handleStateScribingRun / startFeature) breaks exactly 3 tests, and .bee/state.json is already hook-denied for direct edit (guards.mjs:55), so the CLI is a genuine choke point.

decision: chain-integrity D6 — three shipped skills document phase values that DO NOT EXIST in the enum, so their state commands fail 100% of the time: bee-exploring/SKILL.md:73 (--phase exploring-complete), bee-planning/SKILL.md:99 (--phase planning-complete, --phase validated), bee-validating/SKILL.md:82 (--phase validated). Fixed in this feature.
rationale: KNOWN_PHASES (state.mjs:18-29) contains none of these; handleStateSet throws at bee.mjs:755 on any unknown name. An agent following its own skill verbatim hits an error, and then improvises a phase value that DOES pass — which is a direct contributor to the improvised phase writes in the post-mortem. A workflow whose documented commands fail teaches the agent to freestyle the state machine.
alternatives: Adding these names to the enum: rejected — they are stage-completion markers, not phases, and the phase field already carries the completion meaning (planning-complete is just phase=planning with the gate approved).

Added test_bee_cli.mjs + scripts/test_verify_manifest.mjs to verify; test_verify_manifest reads .bee/config.json and fails if any of the 7 required suites (test_lib, test_onboard_bee, test_portable_paths, test_model_guard, test_write_guard, test_hook_contracts, test_bee_cli) is dropped, and carries an internal self-test proving its own checker flags a verify-string missing test_bee_cli. Prove the fix by flipping test_split_brain_regression.mjs from exit 3 to exit 0.

So honest-drift needs an authoritative-reference design that does not exist yet — building drift on the release manifest was a false premise. The preflight is the primary fix: a refused downgrade leaves .bee/bin/lib intact so drift correctly stays false. Drift-honesty is defense-in-depth for other downgrade paths; it and the classifier need their own validated slice. Total autopilot: recorded, not stopped.

decision: perf-log: a global cross-project performance log lives at ~/.config/beehive/performance.jsonl (append-only JSONL, one section per line; respect XDG_CONFIG_HOME, override via BEEHIVE_PERF_DIR). Each section is tagged with project path, git branch, session id, and start/end timestamps.
rationale: User wants one shared location so multiple projects all write their performance sections into the same place; JSONL append is the existing bee I/O idiom (fsutil.appendJsonl) and stays machine-readable + human-renderable.
alternatives: Per-repo .bee/ log (rejected: user explicitly wants cross-project global); single markdown file (rejected: not append-safe/parseable, so we render markdown on demand instead).

decision: perf-log data source & metrics: each section is computed by slicing the current Claude Code session transcript (~/.claude/projects/<encoded>/<session>.jsonl) to the section's [start,end] window. Per model, dedupe assistant events by top-level requestId, then sum message.usage.{input_tokens, output_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens, cache_read_input_tokens}; report new = input+output+cache_creation and cached = cache_read plus a total. Subagent cost + parallelism come from <session>/subagents/agent-*.jsonl (isSidechain) + .meta.json. Parallel = true when >=2 subagents overlap in time or >=2 Agent tool_use blocks share a turn. Model <synthetic> is excluded.
rationale: Verified by two I/O workers reading real transcripts: usage/model/timestamp/requestId/subagent-sidecar fields all present and stable; the agent cannot reliably self-report mid-flight token counts, so post-hoc transcript slicing at section-close is the only trustworthy source.
alternatives: Live per-turn hook accumulation (rejected: CC hooks don't expose per-message usage reliably and add per-turn overhead); trusting the model to self-report tokens (rejected: not available/reliable).

decision: perf-log running time = active execution time, not wall-clock 'alive' time. Compute it by summing the harness-emitted system/turn_duration events' durationMs within the window (harness already excludes idle waiting); fallback when absent = sum of consecutive-event timestamp gaps below an idle threshold (default 300s).
rationale: User asked explicitly for 'thời gian chạy, không tính thời gian sống' (running time, excluding idle). The transcript already carries a harness-measured per-turn durationMs that excludes user-wait gaps — authoritative and cheaper than reconstructing spans.
alternatives: end_ts - start_ts wall clock (rejected: includes idle/user-away time the user explicitly does not want); fixed idle-threshold gap-summing only (kept as fallback, not primary, since turn_duration is more accurate).

decision: perf-log section boundaries: v1 ships explicit named spans via a new 'perf' CLI group (bee perf start --label / bee perf stop --note), universal to any project; the open-section marker is stored per-repo in .bee/perf-open.json and records the resolved session transcript path (newest-mtime, overridable by --session) so stop reads the same file. Auto-emitting a section when a bee cell caps (claim->cap window) is a deferred follow-on slice, not v1 core.
rationale: Explicit spans work everywhere (incl. non-bee projects) and avoid forcing the user to choose a single sectioning model; cell-cap auto-emit is bee-specific sugar that can layer on later without reworking the core aggregation.
alternatives: Section == bee cell only (rejected: not universal, excludes non-cell work); section == whole session (rejected: too coarse for 'what work was done').

decision: Fix (xwh-2, commit 41d5af3, post-cap): reservations release without --cell must scope the cross-worktree ledger release to the acting agent's own cell(s), never a bare {holder, cell:null}
rationale: Live-caught during xwh-2 close-out: a mirrored hold has no agent field (only holder/session/cell), so in an ordinary checkout every agent shares holder='main'. My own close-out 'reservations release --agent exec-xwh2' (no --cell) called releaseHolds({holder:'main', cell:null}), which released ALL of holder main's ledger entries, wrongly clearing 8 of concurrent worker exec-cs2a's (cell cs-2b) still-active mirrored holds. Local per-agent reservations.json rows were unaffected (release() there already filters by agent), so the actual same-checkout conflict guard was never compromised, and no linked worktree was live this session to be misled by the stale ledger — but the bug was real and would bite the feature's actual purpose. Fixed by deriving the acting agent's own active cell id(s) from reservations.json BEFORE release() runs, and scoping releaseHolds to exactly those cells instead of a blanket null. Verified with a new regression test (two different agents/cells sharing holder main; releasing one never touches the other) plus a full green run_verify.mjs.
